questions.question: Which four main areas does network management cover according to the guide?

choices.text: Configuration; Performance & Faults; End User Support; Cost.
Configuration; Patch Management; Cloud Migration; Monitoring.

questions.explanation: The document lists Configuration; Performance & Faults; End User Support; and Cost as the four main areas.

questions.question: Historically, who typically managed voice (telephones) and who managed data (computers)?

choices.text: Voice by IT, data by Facilities.
Voice by Facilities, data by IT.
Both by a central Cloud team.
Voice by Vendors, data by Users.
Voice by HR, data by Security.

questions.explanation: The text states voice (telephones) were managed by Facilities and data (computers) by IT.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a benefit of integrating voice, data, LANs and WANs?

choices.text: Removes the need for any written charter or long-range goals.
Requires managers to compromise local policies for site-wide goals.

questions.explanation: The guide actually says success requires a written charter and long-range goals; it does not remove that need.

questions.question: What does configuration management primarily track?
questions.explanation: Configuration management tracks hardware, software and users, and keeps documentation updated with every change.

questions.question: What is the primary use of a login script for a new user?

choices.text: To automatically map drives or printers at login.
To update the operating system kernel.
To reboot the server every morning.
To encrypt all user files by default.

questions.explanation: The document says login scripts execute commands at login to map drives or printers for new users.

questions.question: What does ESD stand for in the context of software updates?
questions.explanation: ESD stands for Electronic Software Distribution used to push updates automatically over the network.

questions.question: What is the Desktop Management Interface (DMI) used for?

choices.text: A standard framework for managing remote configurations.
A wireless protocol for mobile phones.

questions.explanation: DMI provides a standard framework for managing remote configurations like software distribution.

questions.question: Which of these items is explicitly recommended in the hardware documentation list?

choices.text: Network diagrams, serial numbers, vendors, purchase dates, warranty info, and repair contacts.
Future product roadmaps from unrelated vendors.

questions.explanation: The guide lists detailed hardware documentation such as diagrams, serial numbers, vendors, purchase dates, warranties, and repair contacts.

questions.question: What is the main role of a Network Operations Center (NOC)?

choices.text: Designing the user interface for all business apps.
A centralized facility that monitors the network and fixes issues before users notice.
Handling payroll and benefits for network staff.
Creating marketing materials for network vendors.

questions.explanation: A NOC is a centralized facility using specialized software to monitor the network and correct problems early.

questions.question: Which of these is an example of physical statistics collected by the NOC?

choices.text: Hardware data from modems, multiplexers, and circuits.

questions.explanation: Physical statistics refer to hardware data such as from modems, multiplexers, and circuits; logical stats include response times and traffic volumes.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a core purpose of trouble tickets?

choices.text: Problem Tracking (assigning personnel and tracking timelines).
Problem Statistics (analyzing which hardware fails often).
Automatic hardware replacement without human approval.
Management Reporting (evaluating availability and vendor responsiveness).

questions.explanation: The four core purposes are tracking, statistics, prioritization, and reporting; automatic replacement without approval is not listed.

questions.question: In the sample trouble log, out of 88 calls, how many were about client computers?
questions.explanation: The example states that out of 88 calls, 49 were about client computers while only 2 were about external carriers.

questions.question: What availability target range is mentioned for networks?
questions.explanation: The guide specifies a target availability range of 99% to 99.5%.

questions.question: How is monthly availability calculated in the guide?
questions.explanation: The calculation shown is hours available divided by 720 monthly hours (30 days × 24 hours).

questions.question: What does MTBF measure?

choices.text: Average time to repair a failed component.
Mean Time Between Failures: hours or days of continuous operation before a breakdown.
The number of failed components per month.
Total daily traffic divided by time.
The required vendor SLA response time.

questions.explanation: MTBF is the hours or days of continuous operation before a breakdown; higher MTBF indicates better reliability.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the MTTR phases listed?

choices.text: All three of A, B and C are actual MTTR phases.

questions.explanation: MTTR includes MTTDiagnose, MTTRespond, and MTTFix; MTTReport is not part of MTTR.

questions.question: Why are daily statistics plotted on Quality Control Charts?

choices.text: To entertain NOC staff during downtime.
To hide performance issues from management.
To predict failures and plan hardware upgrades by comparing against acceptable limits.
To replace the need for SLAs.

questions.explanation: The guide says daily stats are plotted on Quality Control Charts against upper and lower limits to predict failures and plan upgrades.

questions.question: Why would TCP/IP QoS be used to prioritize videoconferencing traffic?

choices.text: Because a delayed video call greatly degrades the call, while a delay in file download is less noticeable.
Because videoconferencing uses less bandwidth than file downloads.
Because QoS encrypts the video stream by default.
Because file downloads are always blocked when QoS is enabled.
Because QoS reduces CPU utilization on servers.

questions.explanation: The text explains that delay will ruin a video call but won't be noticed in a file download, therefore QoS prioritizes video traffic.

questions.question: Which of these sample SLA metrics appears in the document?

choices.text: Only uptime measured in packet counts.
No response time guarantees at all.

questions.explanation: The sample SLA shown lists 99% availability, under 2 minutes response time, and MTBF greater than 120 days.

questions.question: Which category is usually the hardest to resolve?

choices.text: Software problems (hidden bugs or incompatible settings).
Printer paper jams that are obvious and quick to fix.

questions.explanation: The guide notes software problems are typically the hardest to resolve due to hidden bugs and incompatible settings.

questions.question: Which tier is said to solve about 85% of common everyday issues?

choices.text: No tier solves issues; all are automated.

questions.explanation: The document states Level 1 help desk solves approximately 85% of everyday issues.

questions.question: Which training method is NOT mentioned in the guide?

choices.text: Mandatory weekly certification tests with external proctors.
Ongoing training as roles and software change.

questions.explanation: The guide mentions in-class, one-on-one instruction and manuals; it does not require mandatory external proctored weekly certification tests.

questions.question: Which statement correctly contrasts TCO and NCO?

choices.text: TCO is always lower than NCO because it excludes user productivity.
TCO (~$5,000–$10,000/year) includes wasted user productivity; NCO (~$1,500–$3,500/year) excludes it.
NCO includes hidden productivity losses while TCO does not.
Both TCO and NCO refer to network uptime percentages.
TCO measures only hardware purchase price.

questions.explanation: TCO measures the full annual cost including wasted productivity ($5k–$10k); NCO measures direct hard costs excluding wasted time ($1.5k–$3.5k).

questions.question: For a typical 100-user network, which expense category is the largest portion of the budget?
questions.explanation: The guide states personnel costs typically make up 50–70% of the budget for a 100-user network.

questions.question: Which pair of technologies is given as examples of automation that save hundreds of hours?

choices.text: ESD for software and DHCP for IP addresses.
Manual CD installs and static IP assignment.
Hiring more technicians to manually update each PC.
Replacing NOC with individual user monitoring apps.

questions.explanation: The guide cites Electronic Software Distribution (ESD) and DHCP as automation examples that eliminate many hours of manual labor.

questions.question: What cost benefits do thin clients provide?

choices.text: They require more local support than full desktops.
They reduce software licensing and local support costs.
They are primarily used to increase power consumption.
They replace the need for a NOC.

questions.explanation: Thin clients are lightweight systems that reduce software licensing and local support costs.

questions.question: Charge-back policies (charging departments for their IT usage) are noted as being:

choices.text: Easy to implement for LANs but impossible for WANs.
Common for easily measured WAN/Mainframe usage, but harder to implement for LANs.
Only used for cloud billing and never for on-premise services.
A replacement for standardization and automation.

questions.explanation: The document says charge-back policies work well for WAN/Mainframe usage where usage is measurable, but are harder for LANs.

questions.question: Which statement correctly describes the difference between fault management and incident management?

choices.text: Fault management handles disasters; incident management handles printer jams.
Fault management handles daily breakdowns; incident management handles disasters like major failures or security breaches.
They are identical and interchangeable terms.
Incident management is only about marketing communication after an outage.
Fault management is only run by HR.

questions.explanation: The guide states fault management deals with daily breakdowns while incident management addresses disasters such as security breaches or natural disasters.

questions.question: What is the primary goal of Incident Management?

choices.text: Minimize business impact and restore normal operations quickly.
Never document incidents to protect company image.

questions.explanation: The document clearly states the primary goal is to minimize business impact and restore normal operations as quickly as possible.

questions.question: Which of these is one of the five key elements of a successful incident response?
questions.explanation: The five elements listed include Detection and Identification, Assessment and Prioritization, Response, Resolution and Recovery, and Documentation and Learning.

questions.question: Which stage is part of the core ITSM lifecycle?

choices.text: Office layout redesign unrelated to IT.

questions.explanation: Service Strategy is one of the stages in the ITSM lifecycle (Service Strategy, Service Design, Service Transition, Service Operation, Continual Service Improvement).

questions.question: Which of the following frameworks is recommended as a guide for ITSM practices in the document?
questions.explanation: The document cites ITIL, COBIT, and ISO/IEC 20000 as established ITSM frameworks organizations can follow.
